perturbation.original_text: For a period of three (3) years from the date of disclosure, Recipient agrees to use the same care and discretion to avoid disclosure, publication, or dissemination of Discloser's Confidential Information as it uses with its own similar information that it does not wish to disclose publish, or disseminate, but in no event shall Recipient use less than reasonable care to protect Discloser's Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: For a period of three (3) years from the date of disclosure, Recipient agrees to use some degree of care and discretion to avoid disclosure, publication, or dissemination of Discloser's Confidential Information as it deems appropriate.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "the same care and discretion" to "some degree of care and discretion" and removing the objective standard of 'reasonable care' introduces ambiguity and makes the protection of confidential information subjective and potentially inadequate. Deeming something 'appropriate' is a subjective measure.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text is ambiguous regarding the level of care required to protect trade secrets, potentially falling short of the 'reasonable efforts' standard implicitly required to maintain trade secret status under the Utah UTSA. The UTSA expects trade secret holders to take active measures to protect their information, which the vague language undermines.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Utah Code § 13-24-2 (2022) - Definitions. :: 2022 Utah Code :: U.S. Codes and Statutes :: U.S. Law :: Justia There Is a Newer Version of this Section 2024 Utah Code 2023 Utah Code 2022 Utah Code (here) View All Versions View Our Newest Version Here 2022 Utah Code Title 13 - Commerce and Trade Chapter 24 - Uniform Trade Secrets Act Section 2 - Definitions. Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-2 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-2. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" me
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the requirement to protect confidential information, potentially falling short of what is required to maintain trade secret status. The original text requires "the same care and discretion" and "no less than reasonable care". The revised text uses "some degree of care and discretion as it deems appropriate." This shift introduces ambiguity. Utah Code § 13-24-2 defines misappropriation and mentions 'a duty to maintain secrecy'. The changed text's ambiguity undermines the clarity of this duty, creating a contradiction. The original text ensures a baseline of 'reasonable care', which aligns with the implicit expectation of 'reasonable efforts' to protect trade secrets, while the changed text introduces subjective discretion that could fall below this standard.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient may disclose Confidential Information to its employees and information which may be retained in non-tangible form by persons who have had access to the Confidential Information, including without limitation general ideas, concepts, know-how provided that prior agreement with such parties sufficient to require that party to treat the Confidential Information in accordance with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may generally disclose Confidential Information to its employees, provided there is some understanding that confidentiality should be maintained.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement for a 'prior agreement' and replacing it with 'some understanding' significantly weakens the obligation to ensure employee confidentiality. The original text, while potentially weak, at least requires a prior agreement sufficient to bind the employees. The new text does not.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text doesn't guarantee adequate protection of trade secrets as mandated by Utah UTSA. 'Some understanding' is far less enforceable than a formal agreement. Failing to secure confidentiality agreements with employees having access to trade secrets weakens the measures taken to protect the trade secret, jeopardizing its status under the UTSA.

Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-2 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-2. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" me
perturbation.justification: The change from 'prior agreement with such parties sufficient to require that party to treat the Confidential Information in accordance with this Agreement' to 'some understanding that confidentiality should be maintained' significantly weakens the obligation to protect trade secrets. Utah Code § 13-24-2(2)(b)(ii)(B) defines 'misappropriation' as disclosure or use of a trade secret without consent by a person who knew his knowledge was 'acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy'. A 'prior agreement' clearly establishes such a duty, whereas 'some understanding' is ambiguous and doesn't guarantee a legally enforceable duty of confidentiality, making it easier to misappropriate a trade secret.

perturbation.original_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, or earlier upon Discloser's request, Recipient shall promptly return or destroy all documents and tangible items in its possession which contain any part of the Confidential Information of Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon termination of this Agreement, or earlier upon Discloser's request, Recipient should endeavor to return or destroy documents and tangible items containing Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "shall promptly return or destroy" to "should endeavor to return or destroy" weakens the obligation to return or destroy confidential information upon termination. 'Endeavor' implies an attempt rather than a strict requirement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Failure to promptly return or destroy confidential information after termination, or at Discloser's request, puts the trade secret at risk. The modified text does not mandate this action. This is not sufficient to protect the confidential information. This undermines the measures expected under Utah UTSA for trade secret protection.

Previous Next 13-24-2. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" me
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, creating a legal contradiction. The original text states the recipient 'shall promptly return or destroy' confidential information, a clear directive. The changed text uses 'should endeavor to return or destroy', which only suggests an attempt, not a strict requirement. The Law Explanation supports that the changed text doesn't mandate the action needed to protect the confidential information. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines 'misappropriation' and states that improper means includes 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By weakening the obligation to return or destroy confidential information, the modified text increases the risk of misappropriation. This creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient certifies that the Confidential Information will only be used for the purposes expressly stated herein and will not be rented, leased, sold, sublicensed, assigned, or otherwise transferred.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient intends that the Confidential Information will only be used for the purposes stated herein, with best efforts not to be rented, leased, sold, sublicensed, assigned, or otherwise transferred.
perturbation.explanation: Changing "certifies that the Confidential Information will only be used" to "intends that the Confidential Information will only be used" weakens the commitment, replacing a guarantee with an intention. The addition of 'with best efforts' makes the restriction on transfer also subject to interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The change weakens the explicit control of trade secrets. The 'best efforts' standard is weak and undermines the measures required to protect trade secrets under the Utah UTSA. If recipient fails to keep that promise, it may imply the information was not a trade secret.

Universal Citation: UT Code § 13-24-2 (2022) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next 13-24-2. Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context requires otherwise: (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) at the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his knowledge of the trade secret was: (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) before a material change of his position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" me
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to protect confidential information, potentially undermining trade secret protection under Utah law. The original text states the Recipient "certifies" the information will only be used for express purposes. The changed text uses "intends" and "best efforts", which introduce ambiguity. The Utah Code § 13-24-2 defines 'misappropriation' as disclosure or use of a trade secret 'without express or implied consent'. By weakening the commitment from a certification to a statement of intent with a 'best efforts' clause, the recipient's obligation to maintain secrecy becomes less strict, potentially implying consent or making it more difficult to prove misappropriation, thus contradicting the intent of trade secret protection.
